# THE BASICS TO WASTEWATER TREATMENT IN THE MINING INDUSTRY

Wastewater treatment plays a vital role in the mining industry, ensuring that harmful contaminants are removed from the water before it is released into the environment. The mining industry faces unique challenges in wastewater treatment because it deals with complex and highly contaminated water sources.

This blog post will outline the basics of wastewater treatment in the mining industry, including the processes involved and the importance of compliance.

![WASTEWATER TREATMENT IN THE MINING INDUSTRY](https://www.brotherfiltration.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/1-2.jpg)

## What is Mining Wastewater Treatment?

[Mining wastewater treatment](https://www.brotherfiltration.com/industry/mining-wastewater/) refers to the process of treating wastewater generated from mining operations to ensure it meets environmental standards before release or reuse.

In the mining industry, water is used for various purposes such as mineral processing and dust suppression. However, the water used in these processes can become contaminated with mineral contaminants and other solids, requiring treatment to remove these impurities.

The treatment of mining wastewater involves various processes such as sedimentation, filtration, chemical precipitation, and biological treatment. These processes help remove suspended solids, heavy metals, and other pollutants present in the wastewater, making it safe for disposal or potential reuse.

![What is Mining Wastewater Treatment?](https://www.brotherfiltration.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/2-1.jpg)

If left untreated, this water source, essential for mining processes, presents a multitude of problems. The acidity resulting from wastewater produced by ore and sediment substances not only raises the concentration of metals in the water but also significantly raises the risk of erosion, leaching, and acid mine drainage. Ultimately, these factors directly impact the environment surrounding your mine.

## How Does Water Treatment Work in Mining?

Water treatment in mining is a complex process that involves multiple stages to ensure the safety of disposed water or its potential reuse. The initial step involves the implementation of conventional treatment processes like sedimentation, flotation, aeration, chemical precipitation, and adsorption. These methods effectively remove suspended solids, organic and inorganic pollutants, and heavy metals from the water.

Furthermore, membrane-based technologies, such as reverse osmosis (RO) and nanofiltration (NF), play a crucial role in eliminating dissolved contaminants from the water. By utilizing these technologies, the water is subjected to an additional treatment after the conventional processes.

![How Does Water Treatment Work in Mining](https://www.brotherfiltration.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/3.jpg)

Another treatment involves filtration to achieve a high-quality effluent. Various filtration media technologies, including gravity, pressure, vacuum, and electrocoagulation filters, are employed based on the specific application requirements. These filters effectively remove any remaining suspended solids, disinfectant chemicals, and impurities that are resistant to other treatment methods.

Once the water has undergone treatment and filtration, it can be safely discharged into the environment. Alternatively, it can be utilized for various purposes within the mining operations. These purposes may include activities like mine processes, dust suppression, irrigation, or reclamation. By ensuring that the water is treated effectively, mining companies can minimize environmental impact and optimize water usage within their operations.

## Necessary Conditions of Mining Wastewater Treatment

Mining wastewater treatment requires certain necessary conditions to effectively manage the quantity and quality of the water. The amount of water involved in mining is dependent on the ground conditions and the level of the underground water table, while the quality of the mine water is influenced by local conditions.

To mitigate environmental impact, mining companies employ water management strategies that focus on modern mining operation aspects. Developing water management plans is vital to control water contamination and prevent the discharge of polluted water into the environment. Global mining companies should take proper measures to treat contaminated mine water and reduce the amount of wastewater released sustainably.

![Mining Wastewater Treatment](https://www.brotherfiltration.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/4-1.jpg)

Innovative methods such as Cleaner Production (CP) or No Waste Technology (LNWT) are being introduced worldwide for mining wastewater treatment. These approaches address the issue of water contamination and aim to ensure cleaner water. Treatment innovations range from active processes, which employ advanced electrocoagulation, sedimentation ponds, membrane filtration, and ion exchange for effective removal of contaminants, to passive processes that rely on wetlands, filtration, and absorption through plants and soils.

## Why do we Need to Treat Mining Wastewater?

Mining wastewater treatment is necessary for several reasons. Firstly, mining activities can lead to the generation of large volumes of wastewater that contain various contaminants, such as suspended solids, heavy metals, chemicals, and organic compounds. If left untreated, these pollutants can have detrimental effects on the environment and human health.

Treating mining wastewater ensures that harmful substances are removed or significantly reduced, minimizing the impact on ecosystems and water sources. It helps to protect surface water bodies, groundwater, and nearby communities from pollution.

Furthermore, treated mining wastewater can potentially be reused for other purposes within the mining operations, such as dust suppression, process water, or even irrigation. This reduces the demand for freshwater resources and promotes sustainable water management practices in the mining industry.

![Why do we Need to Treat Mining Wastewater?](https://www.brotherfiltration.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/5-1.jpg)

Complying with regulatory standards is another critical reason for treating mining wastewater. Mining companies are typically required to meet specific discharge limits and comply with environmental regulations to minimize their environmental impact and protect the surrounding ecosystems.

## So What are the Water Treatment Solutions for Mining?

The need for more effective and affordable water treatment is clear and more urgent than ever before as the mining sector expands, the demand for quarry materials rises, and environmental regulations tighten.

![seawater and brackish water reverse osmosis (SWRO & BWRO), thermal desalination](https://www.brotherfiltration.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/6.jpg)

In terms of operational and financial requirements, seawater and brackish water [reverse osmosis](https://www.brotherfiltration.com/uf-ro-membrane/ro-membrane/) (SWRO & BWRO), thermal desalination, and methods for reusing mine effluents are perfectly suited for the mining industry.

## Water Treatment Application in the Mining Industry

Water treatment applications in the mining industry are crucial to mitigate the environmental impact and ensure sustainable operation. Several key applications include:

### Suspended Solids Removal

Treatment processes like sedimentation and filtration are used to remove suspended solids from mining wastewater. This helps reduce turbidity and minimize the risk of clogged pipes and equipment.

### Heavy Metal Removal

Techniques like coagulation, precipitation, and ion exchange are employed to remove heavy metals such as lead, arsenic, and mercury from the water. This prevents their release into the environment, safeguarding water quality.

### PH Adjustment

Mining activities often generate acidic wastewater that can be harmful to aquatic life. pH adjustment through processes like neutralization or chemical dosing helps bring the water to acceptable levels for discharge or reuse.

![Mining activities often generate acidic wastewater that can be harmful to aquatic life](https://www.brotherfiltration.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/7.jpg)

### Cyanide Treatment

In gold mining, cyanide is commonly used as a bleaching agent, and its presence in wastewater poses significant environmental risks. Specialized treatment processes like oxidation or activated carbon adsorption are employed to remove or degrade cyanide compounds.

## Conclusion

### Industry

The ocean occupies most of our planet, and desalination has been used more and more widely over the past 20 years. By using reverse osmosis systems, we help desalination plants to do their job and further help more plants to solve the problem of water shortage.

Every chemical plant is inseparable from industrial water filtration systems. Whether it is the purification and filtration of raw materials and final products or the treatment of industrial wastewater and unwanted chemicals generated during the production process, industrial filtration is a top priority.

Most petrochemical industry products are stored in large containers, such as diesel, gasoline, and fuel oil. This type of storage is highly likely to lead to impurities in the products and affect the product quality. Our mission is to offer efficient and reliable filtration systems for the petrochemical industry so that purification is not a big challenge.

The extremely rapid growth of the pharmaceutical industry has created a greater demand for industrial filtration. Whether it’s upstream cell fermentation, purification, or filtration, we can provide the solution. In addition, We are committed to filtration and separation, protecting the reliability and safety of production equipment.

The food and beverage industry is closely related to the daily life and health of each of us. Therefore, we must meet higher standards to filter these products. As your experts, we help you improve product quality, reduce production costs, and safeguard everyone’s health.

Nature itself has the ability to treat wastewater. But with the huge increase in human production and living activities, it can no longer handle such large amounts of wastewater. We, as industrial treatment producers, help nature to share the burden of wastewater treatment.

Industrial wastewater from mining activities contains high volumes of heavy metals, particulates, and residual chemicals. Wastewater must be purified and filtered in order not to pollute our water resources. We help your plant meet wastewater discharge standards.

In order to extract oil more efficiently and conveniently, large amounts of water must be injected underground to ensure water pressure. And since precious water resources are limited, our technology allows wastewater to be purified so that it can be reused.

When it comes to marine filtration, the first scene that appears in people’s is usually the sea or seawater. But marine filtration is more than seawater. A crucial resource on this planet is seawater. Large amounts of seawater are needed to generate fresh water for drinking and irrigation in locations with a lack of fresh water, just as ships sailing on the ocean need a lot of it for ballast water to keep the ship balanced. Marine filtration is not only about the seawater, but also the transported seawater. The ballast water is a kind of transportation of water. The cargo ships load the seawater from one place and discharge it at another port. The seawater traveled with the cargo ship in ballast tanks. This is the point many people will be indifferent about, the filtration before the discharge of the ballast water. But why do we need filtration for ballast water? it is just the seawater discharge into seawater. Filtration can help to extend equipment service life and help equipment stay at optimum level. Based on their different filtration needs and characteristic, they have respective suitable filtration elements. # Mine Water Treatment

![Mining-header]()

#### Veolia Water Technologies partners with exploration, engineering, and mining companies to solve their mine water challenges, from production of potable water or desalinated water in remote locations to process water and mining wastewater treatment solutions for all types of hard and soft rock mining sites including solution mining.

We provide a full range of mining water treatment and filtration services including preliminary and detailed engineering, standard and custom water treatment equipment, project management, and maintenance support as well as long-term operation.

#### Meeting Your Needs:

![Mining Case Study - Thumb](/sites/g/files/dvc3601/files/styles/default/public/image/2022/02/Mining%20Case%20Study-thumb_0.jpg.webp?itok=J4oiIeRa)

### 

### 

### 

### See how Veolia Water Technologies are supporting mining clients with a wide range of technology, project, and service offerings.

### [Click Here to Download](https://www.veoliawatertech.com/sites/g/files/dvc3601/files/document/2022/02/Veolia%20Mining%20Case%20Study%20Compilation.pdf)

**Applications**

**Services**

#### Pre-Treatment

![](/sites/g/files/dvc3601/files/styles/default/public/image/2020/05/LAN_057_retouched-small.jpg.webp?itok=uUI_9i6Z)

Regardless of the source of the intake water, Veolia's mine water treatment technologies ensure there is a consistent supply of water to the facility.

#### Leach Liquor Filtration

Veolia’s [precoat filtration](/en/solutions/technologies/whittier-filtration/merrill-crowe "Merrill-Crowe ") and vacuum deaeration technologies help gold and silver producers achieve maximum efficiency in their gold and silver recovery operations. By filtering the cyanide leach solution before zinc precipitation, precipitated impurities that could be carried over from the decantation steps are removed. No more than a trace of oxygen is left, minimizing zinc and cyanide consumption.

#### Wastewater Treatment

Veolia is experienced in helping clients reduce their financial exposure and comply with discharge requirements and stringent environmental regulations regarding water management.  We understand the unique requirements of mining operations and our worldwide expertise in mine water issues allows us to offer specialized water treatment solutions that treat water and efficiently remove a wide range of contaminants such as heavy metals and minerals, arsenic, suspended solids, organics, iron, manganese, and specific ions that can impact the local environment.

#### Tailings Pond

Veolia offers a unique combination of water and solid waste treatment capabilities, which enable us to tailor solutions to each mine’s tailings challenge. Veolia ensures overflow compliance and water treatment, but we also look at the overall wastewater strategy. In particular, Veolia investigates opportunities to treat wastewater at source point to reduce the load going to tailings and we see tailings as an opportunity for water and metals recovery to make the most from the available resources.

#### Acid Mine Drainage

Operating mines, as well as abandoned mine sites, generates acid mine drainage which requires water treatment to prevent adverse impacts to rivers and streams. Veolia has developed a proprietary process known as [**DenseSludge™**](http://watertechnorthamerica.veolia.acsitefactory.com/sites/g/files/dvc3601/files/document/2020/06/DenseSludge%20Brochure.pdf) to reduce sludge management costs and improve the operating efficiency of treatment plants. The process utilizes conventional equipment combined with our process expertise in sludge recycle techniques to form a heavier, denser sludge. This solution reduces sludge volume in the clarifier by up to 90% and improves lime utilization, resulting in significant savings.

#### Solutions Mining and/or Resource Recovery

Veolia provides the expertise required for several mining production processes. [**HPD® evaporation and crystallization**](/en/solutions/technologies/hpd-evaporation-crystallization/mining-hpd "Mining - HPD ") systems are often a critical component in evaporites and solution mining, by-product recovery, and internal water process management for the mining industry.

#### Services

Veolia Water Technologies offers a wide range of mining water treatment service options for mines including[**Hubgrade**](/en/solutions/services/hubgrade-digital-services "Hubgrade Digital Services")**™** digital services to monitor your mine water from a distance and [**mobile water and wastewater processing treatment solutions**](https://info.veoliawatertech.com/en-us/mobile-water-treatment-mining) to cover emergency, temporary or long-term water treatment needs.

![Mining technomap](/sites/g/files/dvc3601/files/styles/default/public/image/2022/01/technomap-web-transparent-min.png.webp?itok=XIkXXZEK)

#### Focusing on system performance, compliance with process and environmental requirements and long-term cost-effectiveness, we are able to meet the most demanding standards of reliability, safety and quality.

#### Veolia Water Technologies has proven expertise within a variety of different mine types including:

**Case Study - Canadian Gold Mine**

![](/sites/g/files/dvc3601/files/styles/default/public/image/2020/11/Brucejack%20construction%20image.jpg.webp?itok=1GLz0-26)

**Technology: [Actiflo](/en/solutions/technologies/actiflo "ACTIFLO®")**® and [**Hydrotech™ Discfilter**](/en/solutions/technologies/hydrotech-discfilter "Hydrotech Discfilter")

**Location:** British Columbia, Canada

A leading gold mine producer needed a wastewater treatment plant to remove Total Suspended Solids (TSS) and some heavy metals from their new gold mine effluent by using as little land space as possible while producing a solid waste. The permanent effluent treatment system consisted of several Veolia proprietary technologies which were selected for their robustness and compactness, starting with the [**Actiflo®**](/en/solutions/technologies/actiflo "ACTIFLO®") high-rate ballasted clarification process for primary metals removal.  The water was further polished by a highly-efficient [**Hydrotech™ Discfilter,**](/en/solutions/technologies/hydrotech-discfilter "Hydrotech Discfilter") which gave added insurance in meeting very stringent discharge criteria. A centrifuge was installed to dewater the sludge. The inlet flow was between 3,200 and 6,000 m3/d. 

[**Read Full Case Study**](/en/case-studies/overcoming-metals-and-tss-removal-challenges "Overcoming Metals and TSS Removal Challenges")

## Frequently Asked Questions

### What are the key elements of a mine's water management program?

There are three main elements of a [mine water management program](/en/publications/articles/mine-water-management-best-practices "Mine Water Management Best Practices"): water quantity, water quality and closure plans.  
  
**Water quantity**  
The quantity of water available at a mine site varies depending on the region. For example, in the western United States, water scarcity can play a role where the mine is reusing its pit water, but in parts of Canada, we are seeing the opposite where some mines have a positive water balance and have too much. Another thing to keep in mind is how climate change is going to impact the site’s water balance. This must be taken into account when determining the program. And finally, it is important to find the right balance between treatment capacity and storage needs: increased active treatment capacity reduces storage capacity and vice versa.  
  
**Water quality**  
What is the quality of the source water and how does the waste rock and tailings management impact the water quality? This along with the environmental regulations for effluent discharge are key in selecting the right technology for water treatment. It’s important to anticipate potential future regulations. For example, in British Columbia, Canada, regulations for selenium are becoming more and more stringent; mining companies are anticipating future regulations that may come into play in the near future, so they are already planning for these tighter regulations when developing their mine water treatment programs.  
  
**Closure plans**  
An often forgotten area when developing a mine water management program is to also keep in mind its closure plan. What is needed on a day-to-day basis for operations during the actual life of the mine is different from the actual post-closure operations.

### What is acid rock / mine drainage?

Acid Rock Drainage (ARD) is a naturally occurring process that generates acidic (low pH) and metal-bearing solutions from reactions of certain minerals in rock material in the presence of oxygen (i.e. air) and water. While ARD is a natural process, its occurrence may be intensified by human activities such as road construction which disturb the rock. It is a common problem at mine sites that have sulfide material in the ore and waste streams (e.g. copper, lead/zinc and nickel sulfide operations, some gold operations, and some coal operations). Acidic solutions generated by ARD dissolve metals from the wastes, including copper, lead, zinc, arsenic, cadmium and iron. Such metals can be extremely toxic to fish and other aquatic life downstream and must be treated.

### How do you treat acid mine drainage?

Operating mines, as well as abandoned mine sites, generate acid mine drainage which requires water treatment to prevent adverse impacts to rivers and streams. Veolia has developed a proprietary process known as DenseSludge™ to reduce sludge management costs and improve the operating efficiency of treatment plants that have acid mine drainage.   
  
The DenseSludge™ recycle process forms particles that settle quickly, dewater readily, and hold little water. The result is a reduction in sludge generation by nearly 90% and production of a drier sludge, which reduces the volume of water that returns to the mine pool for re-treatment. The technology has enabled mining companies to extend the life of existing sludge storage facilities and reduce requirements for the building of new storage.

### Why is a water treatment strategy vital for abandoned mines?

The process of dealing with — or remediating — a closed mine site can come with several key issues and challenges, especially from a water treatment perspective. At the end of their productive working lives, it is vitally important to ensure that mines are managed in a manner that mitigates or eliminates any potentially adverse effects. In practice, this means that the owners of individual abandoned or closed mine sites often have a duty of care to protect the natural environment — both in and around the area. They need to make every effort to eliminate the possibility of any negative impacts, which can include contamination of the surrounding land and surface watercourses, as well as the pollution of local groundwater supplies and drinking water aquifers.  
  
In doing so, the deployment of an effective water treatment strategy is likely to form a key component for effective mine closure and reclamation. Contaminated water at closed mine sites can contain a range of harmful and toxic substances and dangerous chemicals, as well as metals such as cadmium, nickel, iron, copper and zinc. Such materials will tend to vary depending on the nature and location of the mine site. Furthermore, water at abandoned mine sites can be highly acidic, which can pose a dangerous threat to human, animal and plant life.

### What is mining wastewater?

Various processes throughout the mining value chain – ore extraction, mineral processing and refining operations – generate substantial quantities of wastewater, some of it in the form of concentrated and highly polluted streams. Stringent regulations require these different types of wastewater to be treated before discharge to the environment. The treatment complexity of these wastewaters varies widely depending on their origin and the nature and quantity of contaminants they contain, either from the original ore, the mine environment, or the treatment processes employed.  
  
Some highly toxic contaminants, such as cyanide, selenium, arsenic, cadmium, mercury and radioactive elements, may be found in wastewater from certain mining operations. Water from some hydrometallurgical processes such as leaching may be particularly heavily contaminated and need intensive treatment to filter the solution. In some cases such as gold cyanidation, appropriate filtration can clean the solution sufficiently to permit in-process reuse and minimize the impact on the WWTP. Pollution abatement processes to control gas or fume emissions in pyrometallurgy also result in water containing high concentrations of contaminants. However, it should be noted that some contaminated streams may contain substances that have economic value when recovered as by-products during treatment.  
  
Some wastewater streams may be treated at source point for direct reuse onsite in another process, while others have to be routed to the wastewater treatment plant or to tailings ponds. In many cases, the largest volumes of wastewater end up in tailings ponds

enriched_points.expanded_content: Gold mining wastewater typically contains heavy metals like arsenic, zinc, and aluminum, as well as cyanide and suspended solids, posing significant environmental risks.
Rhizofiltration utilizes plant roots to absorb and concentrate contaminants from wastewater, effectively removing toxic metals and improving water quality in mining operations.
Recent case studies show significant reductions in contaminants, with over 90% removal of heavy metals and improved compliance with environmental discharge standards.
Advanced treatment technologies, including evaporation and crystallization, enable the recovery of valuable metals from mining wastewater, enhancing resource efficiency and sustainability.
